creator: wblock@wonkity.com
text:
On Thu, 12 Aug 2010, Pav Lucistnik wrote:

>>> Names ($1) commonly contain spaces, so this is no better than status quo.
>>> Suggestions?
>>
>> Attached patch translates spaces to underlines.  $1, the name, is used
>> as likely to be more descriptive than $4, the binary file name.
>> (Superficially tested, could use more.)
>>
>> --- Mk/bsd.port.mk.orig	2010-08-12 12:04:44.000000000 -0600
>> +++ Mk/bsd.port.mk	2010-08-12 12:19:59.000000000 -0600
>> @@ -6416,7 +6416,7 @@
>>   		${ECHO_CMD} "@cwd ${DESKTOPDIR}" >> ${TMPPLIST}; \
>>   	fi; \
>>   	while [ $$# -gt 6 ]; do \
>> -		filename="$$4.desktop"; \
>> +		filename=`${ECHO_CMD} "$$1.desktop" | ${TR} ' ' '_'`; \
>>   		pathname="${DESKTOPDIR}/$$filename"; \
>>   		categories="$$5"; \
>>   		if [ -z "$$categories" ]; then \
>
> Not sure this is enough, someone can put forward slash or other
> metachars in that field.

Understood.  How about only the first word of the command name?  By 
definition, that's unique and only has valid characters.  Very 
superficially tested:

--- Mk/bsd.port.mk.orig	2010-08-12 12:04:44.000000000 -0600
+++ Mk/bsd.port.mk	2010-08-12 16:19:16.000000000 -0600
@@ -6416,7 +6416,7 @@
  		${ECHO_CMD} "@cwd ${DESKTOPDIR}" >> ${TMPPLIST}; \
  	fi; \
  	while [ $$# -gt 6 ]; do \
-		filename="$$4.desktop"; \
+		filename="`${ECHO_CMD} "$$4" | ${CUT} -f1 -d' '`.desktop"; \
  		pathname="${DESKTOPDIR}/$$filename"; \
  		categories="$$5"; \
  		if [ -z "$$categories" ]; then \
